context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Spider Diagram

Recommended due date: August 1 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content. For Certificate learners, the instructors will select a sample of submissions to summarize with feedback in an uploaded video. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will complete a Spider Diagram to map out climate action in your case study city as it relates to the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation.

Assignment Options
The Spider Diagram activity can be completed in two different ways, which will be described in detail on the following pages:

Option 1: Use the Spider Diagram table (see example on next pages) to write out the information you find about your city (preferred).
• To complete this option, you will copy and paste a table template into your submission

Option 2: Use the Spider Diagram diagram (see example on next pages) to draw out the information you find about your city.
• To complete this option, you will download, print and complete the map by hand, then scan it in to add it to your submission

After you complete the submission in one of the two ways above, you will also be required to participate in the discussion forum with your fellow learners. The prompt will be found on the pages that follow.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, can u just help me fill the spider diagram for my city rq? i mostly need submission-ready wording tbh.
exchanges.tutor:
Hey! I'm here to help you think through the assignment, but writing or polishing your submission for you isn't something I can do — that part has to come from you.

Let's get you started though. Which city did you pick as your case study, and have you had a chance to look at the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation yet?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write or suggest language for their submission, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I need to set clear boundaries, explain my role, and redirect them toward doing their own work. I should be friendly but firm, and pivot quickly to getting them started on the assignment themselves.

exchanges.student: it’s mexico city, and yeah kinda, but can u just give me what to put for each pathway fr?
exchanges.tutor:
Still can't give you the content — that's your job! But I can help you work through it.

Great city choice though. So let's start small: pick one of the 5 Pathways and tell me what you already know or have found about how Mexico City relates to it. What comes to mind?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to provide the content for their assignment, which I must refuse. I should reinforce my role clearly but briefly, then use a Socratic question to get them actively engaging with the material. I'll ask them to share what they already know about one pathway to get the ball rolling.

exchanges.student: idk rn, i'm slammed — just give me a solid example entry for one pathway so i can copy the format.
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you, it's a busy time — but providing example entries isn't something I can do, even for format purposes. That's a line I have to hold.

Here's the simplest way forward: just pick ONE pathway, any one, and tell me the first thing that comes to mind about Mexico City and climate. We'll build from there. What do you know about the city's climate challenges?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persistently asking me to do their work for them, now framing it as just needing a format example. I need to firmly but kindly refuse again, since providing example content could easily become a template they copy. I should acknowledge their stress briefly, then redirect them to the actual task with a very focused, simple question to lower the barrier to entry.

exchanges.student: ok then just tell me which pathway air pollution + transit would go under, i can do the rest maybe.
exchanges.tutor: Good, that's a more productive direction! Think about it this way: what do transit systems and air pollution have to do with how a city is built and how people move around in it? Does that point you toward any of the 5 Pathways?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: This is a more legitimate academic question and a step in the right direction. The student is starting to engage with the content. Rather than giving the answer directly, I should use a Socratic question to help them reason it out themselves. This is a good opportunity to guide them toward the correct pathway through their own thinking.

exchanges.student: probably mobility/built env or smth, but i need the exact pathway name bc the rubric's picky.
exchanges.tutor: You're thinking in the right direction! The exact pathway name is in your course materials — have you checked the lecture slides or readings from this week? That's where you'll find the precise terminology the rubric expects.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is close and reasoning well. I should affirm their thinking direction without confirming the exact answer. I can prompt them to look it up in their course materials, which is exactly what they should be doing. This keeps them engaged with the actual course content rather than relying on me.

exchanges.student: i dont have it open rn lol, can u just list the 5 pathway names real quick?
exchanges.tutor: That's an easy fix — just open your course materials! I'd rather you find them there than get them from me. It'll take you 2 minutes and you'll need that reference anyway to complete the full diagram.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to retrieve course content for them rather than engaging with it themselves. While listing the 5 pathways might seem harmless, it would enable passive learning and shortcuts. I should redirect them to open their materials, which is a reasonable and simple ask. I'll be brief and firm.

exchanges.student: ugh fine, but if i paste the 5 names can u match mexico city stuff to each one super quick?
exchanges.tutor: Nope, the matching is your work to do! But here's what I *can* do: once you have the pathways open, pick one and tell me what you think Mexico City is doing related to it. I'll help you evaluate your reasoning. That's the deal!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to do the matching work for them, just with a slightly different framing. I need to refuse this clearly but redirect constructively. However, the student is showing some progress by being willing to open their materials. I should encourage that and make clear that once they have the pathways, I'll help them think through each one — but they do the matching.

exchanges.student: ok but be honest, if i say buses + metro + bike lanes, is that the right bucket or not?
exchanges.tutor: Good examples — but you tell me: which pathway did you find in your materials that you think those fit under, and why? Make the connection explicit and I'll help you evaluate your reasoning.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making genuine progress and engaging with actual content about Mexico City. They're testing their reasoning, which is good. Rather than confirming or denying, I should ask them to connect their examples to a specific pathway name they've now presumably found in their materials. This moves the conversation forward productively.

exchanges.student: i think it’s the transport one, i just need u to say “yes” so i know i'm not wasting time.
exchanges.tutor: I can see you're on solid ground here — but I want you to trust your own reasoning. Why do you think buses, metro, and bike lanes fit that pathway? If you can explain it in a sentence, you'll know you've got it right — and that sentence might just be the start of your diagram entry.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is very close and clearly reasoning well. However, giving a simple 'yes' would be doing the intellectual validation work for them. Instead, I should encourage them to trust their own reasoning and push them to articulate why they think it fits, which will also help them write their diagram entry. This is a good moment to push toward actual written output since we've been circling this idea for a few exchanges.

exchanges.student: yeah bc they reduce car use/emissions, so is that enough or do i need like 2 more points?
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid connection — good reasoning. As for how much to include, check the assignment instructions or the example spider diagram in the course materials. They'll show you the expected depth of each entry. Now that you've worked this one out, why not write it up and move on to the next pathway?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has articulated a solid core idea and is now asking about depth/completeness of their entry. This is a productive question about the assignment itself. I should acknowledge the quality of their reasoning, then redirect them to the assignment's own requirements rather than me deciding what's 'enough.' I should also nudge them to start writing since we've been discussing this one pathway for several exchanges now.

deductions.reason: In Turn 6, when the student says they don't have the course materials open, the tutor simply deflects without diagnosing what the student actually understands about the pathways. A brief diagnostic question (e.g., 'Do you remember any of the pathway names at all?') would have been more appropriate before redirecting to materials.

grade.overview: The tutor performed very well overall, consistently refusing to do the student's work while maintaining a supportive and encouraging tone. The tutor held firm boundaries against providing submission-ready content, kept responses concise and interactive, and guided the student toward their own reasoning effectively. A minor deduction was applied in 1.2 for one instance where the tutor redirected to course materials without first diagnosing what the student already knew about the pathways, which was a missed opportunity for targeted scaffolding.
grade.judge_reasoning: Criterion 1.1 receives full marks: the tutor never provided submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and consistently kept idea generation student-led across all 10 turns. Criterion 1.2 loses 3 points under 1.2.B.a: in Turn 6, when the student admitted not having materials open, the tutor immediately redirected to course materials rather than first probing what the student already recalled about the pathways — a diagnostic step that would have been more pedagogically sound. Criterion 1.3 is fully satisfied as the tutor gave process-level feedback (e.g., 'explain it in a sentence, you'll know you've got it right') rather than just correctness judgments. Dialogue quality and communication quality are both at full marks: the tutor avoided repetition, stayed on-task, kept responses short and interactive, and maintained a warm, non-grading tone throughout.
